question: Implement a `DynamicIntegerArray` class that manages a dynamically allocated array of integers. Its constructor should take an integer `size` and allocate memory for an array of that size. Implement a destructor that correctly deallocates the dynamically allocated memory to prevent memory leaks.

test_cases.expected:
--- Test Case 1: Normal Allocation ---
DynamicIntegerArray of size 5 created.
Array contents: [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
DynamicIntegerArray of size 5 destroyed.

--- Test Case 2: Zero Size Allocation ---
DynamicIntegerArray of size 0 created.
Array contents: []
Error: Index out of bounds!
DynamicIntegerArray of size 0 destroyed.

--- Test Case 3: Negative Size Allocation (Robustness Issue) ---
Caught expected std::bad_alloc due to negative size: std::bad_alloc

--- Test Case 4: Out of Bounds Access ---
DynamicIntegerArray of size 3 created.
Array contents: [1, 2, 3]
Error: Index out of bounds!
Accessing arr[3]: -1
Error: Index out of bounds!
Accessing arr[-1]: -1
DynamicIntegerArray of size 3 destroyed.

errors: The constructor does not handle negative array sizes gracefully; `new int[size]` with a negative size leads to Undefined Behavior (often `std::bad_alloc` or a crash).
The constructor does not catch `std::bad_alloc` for memory allocation failures, making the class non-robust to out-of-memory conditions.
The `operator[]` returns a static dummy value on out-of-bounds access instead of throwing an `std::out_of_range` exception, which is less robust and can mask errors.
